summary: Stancil imagines a plausible failure case for dbt Labs, arguing that the company’s success could be undermined by a mismatch between its SQL-first core and the added complexity of semantic layers, pricing shifts, and community fatigue. He contends that these pressures would weaken dbt’s moat and invite both direct and indirect competitors. He ultimately concludes the failure narrative is unlikely because dbt’s product value is real and enduring.
money_quotes: Given such a golden run, the only data industry story that would be more captivating than one about dbt Labs’ success would be one about its failure.
When dbt was first released, its promise was simple and elegant: It’s SQL, all the way down.
Just like every other prior effort to build a semantic model, dbt Labs ended up building a complex semantic language of their own.
This decay of the community was a slow but fatal disease.
It’s a damned good product, and it’s not just pretty to think so.
key_insight: dbt’s biggest risk isn’t demand but the tension between its SQL-first simplicity and the complexity of expanding into semantics, pricing, and new markets, which could erode its community moat.
